svg.title: Pick the corner your workload actually needs
svg.desc: Two crossed axes forming four quadrants, one for each dominant cost that Part 5 identified. The upper region is peak throughput for long-running services, where the default JVM with its JIT sits, having paid the warmup tax to get there. The left region is fast cold start, where Rust, Go and native-image sit, since they run at their peak from the first call. The right region is memory density, meaning node cost, where Rust and a Go process with GOMEMLIMIT tuned sit, because their idle fixed cost per pod is a few mebibytes rather than tens. The lower region is low tail latency, where Java's ZGC sits, having bought sub-millisecond pauses by surrendering density and throughput. Each approach reaches one region and no approach reaches them all; the workload's dominant cost decides which corner is the right one.
svg.text: no approach reaches two corners — the workload picks one
